meta.purpose: Codified specification of the three human-computer interaction surfaces: marketing website, CLI/terminal, and web client. Each surface is specified with JTBD, happy paths, sad paths, primitives, and acceptance criteria. This document compiles into design, engineering, and content artifacts.
meta.philosophy: The operator is not a developer. Every surface must be legible to someone who runs business tools daily but has never shipped code. The CLI is the deepest surface; the marketing site is the shallowest. The web client bridges them.
meta.dogfoodingNote: This JSON follows the same spirit as the DESIGN.md spec: human-readable, machine-parseable, token-and-reference based where it makes sense. Prose gives rationale; structured fields are normative.

cx-operator.label: Customer experience lead
cx-operator.technicalFloor: cannot install Node from scratch; can follow copy-paste instructions

gtm-operator.label: GTM lead or solo-founder wearing GTM hat
gtm-operator.budget: activity-driven; HITL tax is the pain, not cost
gtm-operator.technicalFloor: lives in CRM, comfortable with no-code tools
motivators: reclaim HITL time for the calls that matter

marketing-operator.label: Marketing lead running paid + content + social
marketing-operator.stackShorthand: Meta Ads + Google Ads + HubSpot + CMS + Canva
marketing-operator.budget: creative production already a line item; highest token user
marketing-operator.technicalFloor: comfortable with ad platforms and analytics; not engineering

back-office-operator.label: AP/AR, HR, finance ops
back-office-operator.technicalFloor: deeply procedural; high tolerance for structured tools

marketing-website.role: Top-of-funnel conversion surface. Converts awareness into workshop signups, which convert into cohort enrollments, which convert into long-term ecosystem participation.
marketing-website.relationship: Sits alongside chaiwithjai.com; cross-links but not subordinate. teach.chaiwithjai.com handles LMS and cohort delivery.

jobsToBeDone.job: When I hear about prod-init from a friend or content, I want to understand in under 30 seconds whether this is for me, so I can decide whether to go deeper.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: visitor scrolls past hero within 10 seconds OR bounces immediately; bounce rate target <55%

jobsToBeDone.job: When I'm evaluating whether this could work for my specific stack, I want to see my exact tools mentioned, so I believe this was built for me.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: stack-specific landing pages exist for GTM, CX, Marketing; visitor engages with their persona page >60s median time

jobsToBeDone.job: When I'm convinced this is interesting, I want a low-risk way to try it, so I don't have to commit money or a full weekend.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: free workshop signup conversion >15% of persona-page visitors

jobsToBeDone.job: When I've attended the free workshop and shipped something that works, I want a clear next step, so I can go deeper without guessing.
When I'm evaluating the cohort, I want to see real outcomes from real people like me, so I can trust the claim.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: case study page present with ≥3 published studies; each study has persona, stack, measurable outcome

jobsToBeDone.job: When I'm a developer looking at this, I want to find the open-source CLI quickly, so I can evaluate it on technical merits without the course marketing.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: GitHub link in top nav; CLI docs reachable in one click from any page

happyPaths.name: Cold visitor → workshop signup
steps: Lands on homepage from social, newsletter, or podcast mention
Sees hero that states outcome in persona-agnostic terms ('build your own software without becoming a developer')
Scrolls to persona strip, recognizes their role
Clicks through to persona page (GTM, CX, Marketing, Back office)
Reads a specific scenario in their language with their tools
Watches 60-90 second demo video (operator using prod-init against a real stack)
Clicks 'Join the free workshop' CTA
Completes Eventbrite or direct signup in under 90 seconds
Receives confirmation email with calendar invite and pre-workshop prep

happyPaths.name: Workshop attendee → cohort purchase
steps: Attends live workshop (virtual or K Vibe in-person)
Ships a working first workflow during the session
Receives post-workshop email with case study examples and cohort overview
Visits cohort enrollment page
Reviews curriculum, outcomes, prerequisites, refund policy
Checks cohort dates that match their calendar
Clicks enroll, completes Stripe checkout
Receives welcome sequence with pre-work and community invite
happyPaths.duration: typically 2-7 days between workshop and purchase decision

happyPaths.name: Developer → open source CLI evaluation
steps: Lands on site via GitHub trending, dev community share, or search
Notices GitHub badge in nav
Clicks through to repo OR to 'for developers' landing page
Reviews architecture, contribution guide, license
Runs `npx prod-init init` on own machine
Optionally returns to site for docs or issues

sadPaths.name: Visitor bounces because positioning is unclear
sadPaths.trigger: homepage hero fails to convey outcome within 10 seconds
sadPaths.failureMode: visitor assumes this is a developer tool and leaves, or assumes it is no-code and will be too limiting
recovery: A/B test hero headlines with operator-first framing
Add persona selector above the fold
Track scroll depth and bounce by referrer; identify which channels send mismatched audiences
sadPaths.guardrail: weekly review of bounce rate by traffic source; if any source exceeds 70%, pause spend or adjust landing

sadPaths.name: Workshop signup friction
sadPaths.trigger: signup form asks for too much information, payment method unexpectedly requested, or signup flow breaks on mobile
recovery: Minimum fields for free workshop: email + name + role
Stripe/Eventbrite integration tested weekly
Mobile-first form QA on every deploy
sadPaths.guardrail: form completion rate instrumented; alert if drops below 80% of starts

sadPaths.name: Cohort enrollment page misrepresents scope
sadPaths.trigger: visitor purchases expecting something different than what the cohort delivers
sadPaths.failureMode: refund request in week 1-2, negative feedback, potential public complaint
recovery: Explicit 'who this is for / who this is not for' section on enrollment page
Prerequisites clearly stated
Sample curriculum downloadable
Office hours with Jai or coordinator available pre-purchase
sadPaths.guardrail: refund rate >10% triggers enrollment pause and content audit

sadPaths.name: Case study appears fabricated or thin
sadPaths.trigger: case studies lack specifics, metrics, or named persons
sadPaths.failureMode: skeptical visitors bounce; trust erodes; community loses faith
recovery: Every case study requires: named person (with consent), specific stack, measurable before/after, direct quote
Video case studies where possible
Link to LinkedIn profiles for verification
sadPaths.guardrail: no case study publishes without all four required fields; no fabrication of personas

sadPaths.name: Site falls over during viral moment
sadPaths.trigger: traffic spike >10x baseline (HN front page, influential tweet, podcast mention)
sadPaths.failureMode: site slow or down, lost conversions, bad first impression
recovery: Site statically rendered or heavily cached (Astro, Next.js static export, Cloudflare Pages)
Signup forms degrade to email capture if payment/DB are overloaded
Status banner surfaces known issues honestly
sadPaths.guardrail: load test at 20x baseline pre-launch; RB-006 runbook invoked

keyPages.purpose: Hero with outcome, persona strip, social proof, workshop CTA
GTM-specific landing with their stack, their scenarios, their case studies
Case study gallery filterable by persona and stack
Jai bio, Chai With Jai ecosystem, philosophy

marketing-website.designTokenReference: Marketing site design tokens live in a DESIGN.md committed alongside the site repo. Tokens draw from Chai With Jai brand system. Not duplicated here to avoid drift.

qualitative.firstImpressionTest: 5 unseen operators can articulate 'who this is for and what it does' within 30 seconds of viewing the homepage
qualitative.personaFitTest: operators from each target persona recognize themselves on their persona page within 15 seconds

cli-terminal.role: Primary product surface. This is where operators become developers-of-their-own-software. Every operator who becomes a power user spends hours here.
cli-terminal.philosophy: The CLI is not a developer tool pretending to be an operator tool. It is an operator tool that happens to run in a terminal because that gives us power, speed, and Git-native workflows. Every interaction is designed for someone whose first terminal experience was yesterday.

jobsToBeDone.job: When I've decided to try prod-init, I want to get from zero to a running workflow in under 30 minutes without calling a developer friend, so I maintain momentum.
When something goes wrong during setup, I want the error to tell me what to do next in plain language, so I don't get stuck.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: recovery_rate_from_error >=80%; zero raw stack traces surfaced

jobsToBeDone.job: When I want to run a workflow, I want one short command and clear feedback, so I don't have to remember syntax.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: workflow_run_success_rate_p95 >=95%; every run produces a visible outcome

jobsToBeDone.job: When a workflow gives me an unexpected result, I want to see exactly what happened, so I can fix my instructions not debug the tool.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: every run has a human-readable log; structured view available on demand

jobsToBeDone.job: When I want to add a new integration or workflow, I want a guided path that respects my existing setup, so I don't fear breaking things.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: additive commands are non-destructive by default; confirmation required for anything modifying existing state

jobsToBeDone.job: When I want to understand what the tool is doing on my behalf, I want transparency without noise, so I can trust it.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: `--verbose` reveals Claude Code invocations, MCP calls, token counts; default mode stays clean

commandTaxonomy.design: Commands are verbs on nouns. Single word where possible. Every command has a help text, a --json flag, a --dry-run flag where side effects exist, and documented exit codes.

commands.purpose: First-run setup. Installs Claude Code if missing, scaffolds workspace, configures MCP integrations for chosen stack, runs verification workflow.
Diagnose setup problems. Checks Claude Code, network, MCP integrations, git, Node.
List, add, remove, or re-authenticate integrations.
Shorthand for `workflow run`. The most-used command.
View run history. Supports filters by workflow, status, date range.
Contextual help. `prod-init help <command>` for command-specific help.
Upgrade prod-init and workflow templates. Shows changelog before applying.

exitCodeTaxonomy.1: generic error (should be rare; prefer specific codes)
exitCodeTaxonomy.20: workflow error (user workflow issue, not tool issue)

happyPaths.name: First-run setup (operator never used a terminal before)
steps: User runs `npx prod-init init` from a tutorial or docs page copy-paste
CLI detects no Claude Code, explains what it is in one sentence, asks permission to install
User presses Enter to accept
Progress bar for Claude Code install; takes ~60 seconds
CLI prompts for stack choice via arrow keys: GTM, CX, Marketing
User selects GTM
CLI shows which integrations will be set up (HubSpot, Gong, Apollo) and asks for confirmation
User confirms
For each integration: CLI opens browser to OAuth URL, user grants access, CLI verifies with a small test call, marks success
One of three integrations fails auth. CLI marks it 'skipped, retry later', continues.
CLI scaffolds the workspace in `~/prod-init-workspace`
CLI runs a verification workflow (e.g., 'list my 5 most recent HubSpot contacts') to prove end-to-end works
CLI prints summary: 2 of 3 integrations working, 1 to fix, 5 workflow templates installed, next command to try
User runs `prod-init run welcome-tour`, sees their own data come back, experiences the aha moment

happyPaths.name: Running a workflow against real data
steps: User runs `prod-init run triage-tickets`
CLI shows spinner with live status: 'Fetching new Zendesk tickets... 12 found'
CLI streams Claude Code's work: 'Categorizing... drafting responses in your voice...'
Token count ticks up visibly; remains within budget
CLI prints structured output: table of tickets with category, suggested response, escalation flag
CLI offers: '[v] view full log [s] save output [d] deliver to Slack [q] quit'
User picks 'd', output posted to configured Slack channel
Run history updated; cost recorded
happyPaths.duration: 30 seconds to 3 minutes depending on volume

happyPaths.name: Adding a new workflow from a template
steps: User runs `prod-init workflow new`
CLI shows numbered list of available templates for their stack
User picks one by number
CLI asks 3-4 clarifying questions (schedule? output destination? tone of voice?)
CLI scaffolds workflow directory with starter CLAUDE.md, prompts, config
CLI offers: 'edit now, or run first to see defaults in action?'
User picks 'run first'
Workflow executes with defaults, user sees result, then can iterate

happyPaths.name: Recovering from an MCP auth expiring
steps: User runs `prod-init run research-accounts` a week after initial setup
CLI detects HubSpot token expired mid-run
CLI pauses run, surfaces: 'HubSpot auth expired. [r] re-authenticate now [s] skip this step [q] quit and fix later'
User picks 'r'
CLI opens browser, user reauths in ~30 seconds
CLI resumes the run from where it paused (idempotent)
Run completes successfully

sadPaths.name: Node not installed, unclear error
sadPaths.trigger: user runs `npx prod-init init` but Node is missing or outdated
sadPaths.failureMode: naive npx failure produces confusing error; user gives up
recovery: Install script (`curl | sh`) checks Node version, installs via Volta or nvm if missing, explains each step
npm command wrapped to detect Node issues early and show a friendly message with install instructions
Docs prominently feature the install script as the default path, npx as alternative for devs
sadPaths.guardrail: RB-001 runbook: any three-user trend of this failure is a P0 fix

sadPaths.name: Claude Code install fails silently
sadPaths.trigger: Claude Code install step fails due to network, permissions, or upstream issue
sadPaths.failureMode: CLI continues as if installed; workflow runs fail cryptically
recovery: Every Claude Code install has explicit post-install verification (runs `claude-code --version` or equivalent)
If verification fails, CLI surfaces exact error, suggests manual install, offers docs link
CLI refuses to continue until Claude Code is proven working
sadPaths.guardrail: verification step is non-skippable; failure is blocking

sadPaths.name: All three OAuth flows fail in a row
sadPaths.trigger: browser doesn't open, redirect URI mismatch, or provider is down
sadPaths.failureMode: user gets through setup with zero working integrations; experience is hollow
recovery: CLI detects when zero integrations succeed and treats this as a setup failure, not a warning
Offers to retry later with clear command, or to continue with a demo-data workflow so user sees *something* working
Link to troubleshooting page specific to each provider
sadPaths.guardrail: init cannot report 'success' with zero working integrations

sadPaths.name: Workflow produces bad output, user doesn't know why
sadPaths.trigger: Claude Code generated output that is wrong, off-brand, or hallucinated
sadPaths.failureMode: user loses trust in the tool, doesn't know whether issue is their prompt, the template, or the AI
recovery: Every run has a structured log: inputs, prompts used, MCP calls made, tokens, outputs
`prod-init history show <run-id>` renders a readable trace
Workflow templates include troubleshooting notes specific to common failures
Community/Prompt Clinic for peer debugging
sadPaths.guardrail: no run is opaque; every output is traceable to inputs

sadPaths.name: Runaway token cost
sadPaths.trigger: workflow has a loop or overly broad prompt, racks up tokens
sadPaths.failureMode: user sees surprise bill from Anthropic
recovery: Per-workflow token cap in template config (default conservative)
Per-run budget warning at 50% and 80%
Hard stop at 100%, clear message with override option
sadPaths.guardrail: rule #6 (per-user budget caps mandatory); no workflow ships without a cap

sadPaths.name: User customizes template, breaks it silently
sadPaths.trigger: user edits workflow.yaml or CLAUDE.md, introduces a syntax error or logical break
sadPaths.failureMode: next run fails cryptically or behaves unexpectedly
recovery: `prod-init workflow validate <name>` runs before every `run` by default (can skip with flag)
Validation catches YAML errors, missing fields, unreachable prompts
Error messages point to specific line and suggest fix
sadPaths.guardrail: validation runs automatically; errors block execution

sadPaths.name: Hostile or abusive input
sadPaths.trigger: user or upstream data contains prompt injection, attempts to exfiltrate credentials, or malicious content
recovery: Credentials never in prompts; always fetched by Claude Code via MCP at call time
Logs redact credentials by structured schema
Workflow templates include input validation guidance
Rare but severe; RB-005 escalation
sadPaths.guardrail: rules #8 (secrets never in env vars) and #9 (reversible deploys); RB-005 incident response

renderingPrinciples: Use color sparingly; never rely on color alone (accessibility)
Spinners for anything >500ms; progress bars for anything >3s with known duration
Structured tables for lists; prose for narrative output
One emoji maximum per command output (if any); rely on unicode box characters for structure
No ASCII art banners on invocation; respect the operator's terminal real estate
terminalUX.interactivity: Use ink (React for CLIs) for multi-step flows. Single commands stay non-interactive unless needed.
terminalUX.messageTone: Direct, warm, operator-first. 'Let's set up your workspace.' not 'Initializing environment...'
terminalUX.errorMessagePattern: What went wrong (one sentence) → Why it matters (optional) → What to do next (imperative)

performance.coldStart: <2s from command invocation to first feedback
performance.crashRate: <5% across all invocations in first 30 days

learnability.noDeveloperRequired: 90% of users who start init finish without asking for help

web-client.role: Bridge surface. Exists primarily for Stage 2+ (scheduled, team, internal service). For v0.1 MVP, minimal: run history viewer and integration auth manager. Expands in v0.2 when hosted runner ships.
web-client.philosophy: The web client is not where operators build workflows. That stays in the CLI and Claude Code. The web client is where they *see* what their software did, invite teammates, and manage credentials they don't want on their laptop.
web-client.v0_1Scope: Minimal: hosted integration dashboard + run history viewer. Read-only for v0.1. Write operations land in v0.2 with hosted runner.
web-client.v0_2Scope: Scheduled workflows UI, team invites, audit log, billing.

jobsToBeDone.job: When I'm away from my laptop, I want to check whether my scheduled workflow ran, so I can intervene if needed.
When I want to invite a teammate to use or see a workflow, I want a clear sharing model, so I can collaborate without giving away credentials.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: invite flow <2 minutes; permission model explicit and legible

jobsToBeDone.job: When I'm reviewing a run that went wrong, I want to see the same detail I'd see in the CLI history, so I can diagnose without switching tools.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: run detail view includes: inputs, prompt, MCP calls, outputs, tokens, timing; feature parity with `prod-init history show`

jobsToBeDone.job: When I need to fix an expired integration credential, I want to do it from anywhere without being at my terminal, so I can unblock my scheduled workflows quickly.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: re-auth flow works on mobile browser; completes in <2 minutes

jobsToBeDone.job: When I'm managing my subscription or viewing my usage, I want a clear billing dashboard, so I'm never surprised.
jobsToBeDone.acceptanceSignal: current-month usage visible; forecasted bill shown; budget cap configurable

happyPaths.name: Checking scheduled run from phone
steps: Operator receives Slack notification that morning triage workflow completed
Taps link, opens web client in mobile browser
Authenticates via magic link (already logged in from prior session)
Lands on dashboard showing most recent runs
Sees green checkmark on triage run, taps for detail
Scans summary: 14 tickets processed, 3 escalations flagged, $0.47 cost
Closes, satisfied

happyPaths.name: Re-authenticating expired integration from mobile
steps: Scheduled run fails due to HubSpot token expiring overnight
User receives email alert with 'Fix now' link
Opens link on phone, lands on integrations page
Sees red indicator on HubSpot, taps
Mobile-friendly OAuth flow opens, completes auth
Integration returns to green state
Option to 'retry failed run now' shown, user taps
Workflow reruns, succeeds

happyPaths.name: Inviting a teammate (v0.2)
steps: Operator on team tier opens web client
Navigates to Workspace → Members
Clicks 'Invite member'
Enters teammate email, picks role (viewer, runner, admin)
Sends invite
Teammate receives email, follows magic link, sees shared workspace with permissions scoped to role

sadPaths.name: Session expired at critical moment
sadPaths.trigger: user tries to act on alert; magic link session has expired
sadPaths.failureMode: user stuck at login, abandons, issue persists
recovery: Magic links valid for 7 days on trusted devices
If expired, re-auth flow is fast and returns user to their intended destination
Email alerts include a fresh magic link, not a deep-link into the app
sadPaths.guardrail: auth flows tested weekly; session-return-to-intent flow has an integration test

sadPaths.name: Run detail view doesn't render for a specific run
sadPaths.trigger: edge case: very long prompt, special characters, or oversized MCP response
sadPaths.failureMode: blank page or error; user can't diagnose their issue
recovery: Run detail always has a fallback 'download raw JSON' option
Truncation with 'show more' for very long fields
Error boundaries around each panel so one broken panel doesn't blank the page
sadPaths.guardrail: run detail view has error boundaries; fallback raw export always available

sadPaths.name: Teammate gets too much access via invite (v0.2)
sadPaths.trigger: permission model is confusing; admin role granted when viewer was intended
recovery: Default role is always 'viewer'; elevation requires explicit second click
Role changes audit-logged
Admin role requires two-factor or explicit re-auth

sadPaths.name: Billing surprise
sadPaths.trigger: user exceeds plan without clear notification
recovery: Usage email at 50%, 75%, 90%, 100% of plan
Hard stop at plan limit unless user explicitly opts into overage
Usage dashboard always visible in web client
sadPaths.guardrail: RB-004 cost spike runbook; platform-level caps enforce

sadPaths.name: Web client outage while CLI still works
sadPaths.trigger: hosted runner or database down, local CLI unaffected
sadPaths.failureMode: user assumes total outage and stops using CLI too
recovery: Status page on separate infra updated immediately
Email alert to paid users within 10 minutes of confirmed outage
CLI detects web client unavailability and explicitly notes 'local workflows still work'

keyPages.purpose: At-a-glance: recent runs, integration health, usage, alerts
Full run detail: inputs, prompts, MCP calls, outputs, tokens
List of workflows (v0.1 read-only; v0.2 editable)
Team tier only: invite, remove, change role

web-client.designTokenReference: Web client draws from the same Chai With Jai design tokens as the marketing site. A single DESIGN.md lives in the design system repo and is consumed by both surfaces. This is intentional for brand consistency.

crossSurfaceConsistency.brandConsistency: Marketing site, web client, CLI output, email communications, and course materials all follow the Chai With Jai brand system. A single DESIGN.md is the source of truth for colors, typography, voice.

voiceAndTone.principle: Operator-first. Warm, direct, specific. No jargon. No 'just' or 'simply'. No condescension.

examples.good: Your HubSpot token expired. Let's reconnect — takes about 30 seconds.
examples.bad: OAuth token has expired. Please initiate reauthorization.

crossSurfaceConsistency.dataConsistency: Run history shown in CLI (`prod-init history`) and web client (`/runs`) must be identical. Same source of truth, different presentation.
crossSurfaceConsistency.authContinuity: User authenticated in CLI can view their runs in web client without separate login. Magic link or device-linked token model.

openQuestions.question: Does the v0.1 MVP actually need a web client at all, or can we launch CLI-only?
openQuestions.consideration: CLI-only is faster to ship. Web client for run history is nice-to-have, not must-have. Mobile re-auth is the only compelling Stage-1 use case.
openQuestions.tentativeAnswer: Launch CLI-only. Add minimal web client (run history + integrations) alongside v0.2 hosted runner. Cuts scope, accelerates launch.

openQuestions.question: Is terminal UX the right first interface, or should we prototype a native desktop wrapper?
openQuestions.consideration: Some operators will never be comfortable in a terminal regardless of how good the UX is. A native app (Electron or Tauri) that shells out to the CLI could reach a wider audience.
openQuestions.tentativeAnswer: Start CLI-first. If private beta reveals terminal phobia as the blocker, prototype a native wrapper for v0.3. Don't build it speculatively.

openQuestions.question: How do we handle the moment a workflow has outputs a user doesn't like?
openQuestions.consideration: This is where trust is won or lost. Surfacing the full trace helps technical users; might overwhelm operators.
openQuestions.tentativeAnswer: Default view: simple before/after with 'what we did' narrative. Detail view: full trace with prompts, MCP calls, tokens. Progressive disclosure.

openQuestions.question: Should the marketing site domain be prod-init.dev, chaiwithjai.com/prod-init, or a new operator-friendly name?
openQuestions.consideration: Name decision blocks site structure, SEO strategy, and brand positioning.
openQuestions.tentativeAnswer: Unresolved. Must decide before public launch.

openQuestions.question: How do we handle offline or spotty connectivity scenarios for the CLI?
openQuestions.consideration: Many operators travel or work from varying locations. Full offline isn't possible (Claude Code needs API); partial graceful degradation is.
openQuestions.tentativeAnswer: CLI detects offline state and queues workflows locally. On reconnect, offers to run queued workflows. Scope for v0.3.

compilesInto.artifact: Marketing site information architecture and page specs
compilesInto.consumer: designer + frontend developer building the site

compilesInto.artifact: CLI command reference and manual pages

compilesInto.consumer: integration tests for CLI, marketing site, web client

nextActions: Review with one external operator from the target personas. Revise.
Pin the v0.1 scope decision on web client (recommend: defer to v0.2).
Decide on name/domain before implementing marketing site.
Prototype CLI init flow end-to-end on one OS + one stack to pressure-test cli-hp-1.
Draft the DESIGN.md for the marketing site using the spec format provided.
Write the first three case study outlines (even before students exist) as templates.
